questions.question: Government can best be defined as:

options.A: the group of people who rule a country
options.B: the institution through which a state exercises authority and makes and enforces laws
options.C: a system of laws that regulates trade

questions.explanation: Government refers to the institution or machinery through which the state exercises its authority, makes laws, and enforces them to regulate society.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an element of the state?

options.B: Territory

questions.explanation: The four essential elements of a state are: population, territory, government, and sovereignty. A political party is not an element of the state.

questions.question: Sovereignty means:

options.A: the right to vote in elections
options.B: the supreme power of a state to govern itself without external interference

questions.explanation: Sovereignty is the supreme, absolute, and unlimited power of a state to govern itself internally (internal sovereignty) and to be free from external control (external sovereignty).

questions.question: The theory that political authority derives from the consent of the governed is known as:

options.B: Force theory

questions.explanation: The social contract theory (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau) holds that the legitimacy of government is derived from the consent of the governed through an implied or explicit agreement.

questions.question: The principle of separation of powers was propounded by:

options.B: Jean-Jacques Rousseau

questions.explanation: Baron de Montesquieu, in his work 'The Spirit of the Laws' (1748), propounded the doctrine of separation of powers into executive, legislative, and judicial branches.

questions.question: The system of checks and balances ensures that:

options.A: one arm of government does not dominate the others
options.B: the executive is supreme
options.C: the judiciary has the most power

questions.explanation: Checks and balances is a system where each branch of government has powers to limit the other branches, preventing any single branch from becoming too powerful.

questions.question: Power is different from authority in that authority is:

options.A: the ability to compel obedience by force
options.B: legitimate power that is accepted as rightful by those over whom it is exercised

questions.explanation: Authority is legitimate power — power that is recognized as rightful and accepted by the governed. Power without legitimacy is mere force or coercion.

questions.question: Legitimacy in government refers to:

options.A: the legal authority to rule backed by public acceptance
options.B: the ability of government to use force
options.C: the wealth of the ruling party
options.D: the size of the country's military

questions.explanation: Legitimacy refers to the rightful claim to govern — a government is legitimate when its authority is accepted as valid by the citizens it governs, usually through law or elections.

questions.question: A system of government in which supreme power is vested in the people, exercised directly or through elected representatives, is called:

options.B: Monarchy

questions.explanation: Democracy is government by the people — either directly (direct democracy) or through elected representatives (representative/indirect democracy).

questions.question: A government system where power is held by a small privileged group is called:

options.B: Oligarchy

questions.explanation: Oligarchy is rule by a small group of people (usually elite, wealthy, or privileged) who exercise power for their own benefit.

questions.question: A government in which a single ruler exercises absolute power over the state is called:

options.B: Parliamentary democracy

questions.explanation: An autocracy is a system where one person (autocrat/dictator) holds unlimited, unchecked power over the entire government and people.

questions.question: A federal system of government is one in which:

options.A: power is concentrated at the centre
options.B: power is shared between a central government and constituent states or regions
options.D: a single party controls all branches of government

questions.explanation: Federalism divides power constitutionally between a central (federal) government and component units (states/provinces), with each level having specific powers.

questions.question: In a unitary system of government, political power is:

options.A: shared between central and regional governments equally
options.B: concentrated at the centre, with regional governments subordinate to it

questions.explanation: In a unitary state, all constitutional power resides in the central government. Regional governments exist at the pleasure of the central government and may have delegated powers.

questions.question: In a parliamentary system of government, the executive is drawn from and responsible to the:

options.B: Military

questions.explanation: In a parliamentary system, the Prime Minister and cabinet are members of the legislature and remain in office only as long as they retain the confidence of the legislature.

questions.question: In a presidential system of government, the president is:

options.B: accountable to the legislature for remaining in office
options.C: both head of state and head of government, elected separately from the legislature

questions.explanation: In a presidential system, the president is directly elected by the people, serves as both head of state and government, and has a fixed term independent of legislative confidence.

questions.question: Which country operates a constitutional monarchy?

options.B: United States

questions.explanation: The United Kingdom is a constitutional monarchy where the monarch (King/Queen) is the head of state but powers are limited by the constitution and parliament.

questions.question: Totalitarianism is a system of government characterized by:

options.B: total control of all aspects of life by the state under a single party or leader

questions.explanation: Totalitarianism is an extreme form of autocracy where the state seeks to control all aspects of public and private life, suppressing opposition. Examples include Nazi Germany and Stalinist USSR.

questions.question: A constitution is best defined as:

options.A: a set of laws made by parliament
options.B: the body of fundamental rules and principles that govern a state
options.C: the president's speech to the nation

questions.explanation: A constitution is the body of fundamental rules, principles, and laws that define the structure of government, rights of citizens, and the distribution of power in a state.

questions.question: A rigid constitution is one that:

options.A: can be amended by a simple majority in parliament
options.B: requires a special and more difficult procedure to amend than ordinary legislation
options.C: cannot be amended under any circumstances

questions.explanation: A rigid constitution requires a special amending procedure (e.g., two-thirds majority, referendum) making it harder to change than ordinary laws. Nigeria's 1999 constitution is rigid.

questions.question: The United Kingdom operates an unwritten (uncodified) constitution. This means that:

options.B: constitutional rules are found in various sources including statutes, conventions, and common law
options.D: the constitution is stored in a secret location

questions.explanation: An unwritten constitution is not contained in a single document but exists across various sources: Acts of Parliament, judicial decisions, and constitutional conventions.

questions.question: The principle of constitutional supremacy means that:

options.A: the president is the supreme authority
options.B: the constitution is the highest law and all other laws must conform to it
options.C: parliament can pass any law it wishes
options.D: the military can override civilian government

questions.explanation: Constitutional supremacy means the constitution is the supreme law of the land. Any law inconsistent with the constitution is null and void to the extent of the inconsistency.

questions.question: The power of the court to declare laws unconstitutional is called:

options.B: Judicial review

questions.explanation: Judicial review is the power of courts to examine legislation and executive actions to determine if they are consistent with the constitution, and to nullify those that are not.

questions.question: The first constitution to introduce the elective principle in Nigeria was the:

options.B: Richards Constitution of 1946

questions.explanation: The Clifford Constitution of 1922 introduced the elective principle in Nigeria for the first time, allowing a limited number of Nigerians to elect representatives to the Legislative Council.

questions.question: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced:

options.B: the federal structure of government
options.C: regionalism by dividing Nigeria into three regions

questions.explanation: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced regionalism by dividing Nigeria into North, East, and West regions, laying the foundation for Nigeria's federal structure.

questions.question: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 introduced which major constitutional change in Nigeria?

options.B: True federalism with regions having greater autonomy

questions.explanation: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 established true federalism in Nigeria, granting the three regions (North, East, West) greater autonomy with their own governments and legislatures.

questions.question: Nigeria gained independence on:

options.B: January 1, 1960

questions.explanation: Nigeria became an independent nation on October 1, 1960, when it was granted independence from British colonial rule.

questions.question: Nigeria became a republic on:

options.B: October 1, 1963

questions.explanation: Nigeria became a republic on October 1, 1963, when it adopted the Republican Constitution, replacing the Queen as head of state with an executive President (Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe).

questions.question: The first military coup in Nigeria took place on:

options.B: January 15, 1966

questions.explanation: Nigeria's first military coup was the January 15, 1966 coup led by Major Kaduna Nzeogwu, which overthrew the civilian government of Prime Minister Tafawa Balewa.

questions.question: The Nigerian Civil War lasted from:
questions.explanation: The Nigerian Civil War (Biafra War) lasted from July 6, 1967, when the Eastern Region declared independence as the Republic of Biafra, until January 15, 1970, when Biafra surrendered.

questions.question: The current Nigerian Constitution in use was enacted in:
questions.explanation: Nigeria currently operates under the 1999 Constitution (as amended), which was promulgated on May 5, 1999, and came into force on May 29, 1999, when democratic governance was restored.

questions.question: The Nigerian National Assembly consists of:

options.B: The House of Representatives only
options.C: The Senate and the House of Representatives
options.D: The Senate, House of Representatives, and State Assemblies

questions.explanation: The National Assembly of Nigeria is bicameral, consisting of the Senate (upper house, 109 senators) and the House of Representatives (lower house, 360 members).

questions.question: The Nigerian Senate has how many members?
questions.explanation: The Nigerian Senate has 109 senators — 3 from each of the 36 states plus 1 from the FCT (Abuja). This ensures equal state representation in the upper chamber.

questions.question: The primary function of the legislature is to:

options.B: interpret the law

questions.explanation: The primary function of the legislature is law-making (legislation). It also performs oversight, represents constituents, and approves the budget.

questions.question: A bill passed by both chambers of the National Assembly becomes law when it is:

options.B: signed by the President or passed over the President's veto

questions.explanation: A bill becomes law when it is assented to (signed) by the President. If the President withholds assent, the National Assembly can override the veto with a two-thirds majority.

questions.question: The presiding officer of the Nigerian Senate is the:

options.B: Senate President

questions.explanation: The presiding officer of the Nigerian Senate is the Senate President. The presiding officer of the House of Representatives is the Speaker.

questions.question: Legislative immunity (privilege) protects legislators from:

options.B: civil and criminal liability for statements made in the course of legislative proceedings

questions.explanation: Legislative privilege (parliamentary immunity) protects legislators from legal liability for statements, votes, and actions taken during legislative proceedings.

questions.question: Under the 1999 Constitution of Nigeria, the President is elected through:

options.B: direct election by the people

questions.explanation: Under the 1999 Constitution, the President of Nigeria is elected by direct vote of the people, requiring a majority of votes cast and at least 25% in at least 24 states.

questions.question: The Nigerian President serves a term of how many years?

options.B: 5 years, not renewable

questions.explanation: The Nigerian President serves a term of four years and can be re-elected for a maximum of two terms (8 years total), as provided by the 1999 Constitution.

questions.question: The process of removing the President from office before the end of his term through a legislative vote is called:

options.B: Vote of no confidence

questions.explanation: Impeachment is the formal process by which the legislature brings charges against the President (or other officials) for misconduct, potentially leading to removal from office.

questions.question: The cabinet in a presidential system differs from a parliamentary cabinet in that the presidential cabinet:

options.B: is collectively responsible to the legislature
options.C: consists of advisers appointed by and accountable to the president alone
options.D: must be members of the legislature

questions.explanation: In a presidential system, cabinet ministers are appointed by the president, are individually accountable to the president, and need not be members of the legislature.

questions.question: The highest court in Nigeria is the:

options.B: Federal High Court

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of Nigeria is the apex court and the final court of appeal. Its decisions on constitutional matters are binding on all other courts.

questions.question: Judicial independence means that:

options.A: judges can make any decisions they like
options.B: the judiciary is free from interference by the executive and legislature in its decisions
options.C: the courts are not accountable to anyone
options.D: the Chief Justice appoints the President

questions.explanation: Judicial independence means that judges make decisions based solely on law and facts, free from political pressure, influence, or interference from the executive or legislature.

questions.question: The writ of habeas corpus is used to:

options.A: compel a witness to appear in court
options.B: challenge the unlawful detention of a person and require their release

questions.explanation: Habeas corpus (Latin: 'you should have the body') is a writ that requires a person detaining another to bring the detainee before a court to justify the detention.

questions.question: In Nigeria, judges of the Supreme Court are appointed by:

options.B: The National Judicial Council on the recommendation of the President, subject to Senate confirmation

questions.explanation: Under the 1999 Constitution, Supreme Court justices are appointed by the President on the recommendation of the National Judicial Council, subject to confirmation by the Senate.

questions.question: The rule of law, as propounded by A.V. Dicey, means all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: equality before the law
options.C: the government is subject to the law
options.D: the executive makes all judicial decisions

questions.explanation: Dicey's rule of law means: (1) supremacy of law (no one is above the law), (2) equality before the law, and (3) constitutional rights are the result of judicial decisions. Executives cannot make judicial decisions.

questions.question: A political party is defined as:

options.A: a group of people who share the same religion
options.B: an organized group of people with similar political ideologies seeking to gain and exercise political power

questions.explanation: A political party is an organized group of individuals who share similar political views, field candidates for elections, and seek to gain control of government to implement their policies.

questions.question: A two-party system is one in which:

options.A: only two parties are allowed to exist
options.B: two major parties dominate the political system and typically alternate in power
options.C: two presidents rule at the same time
options.D: a coalition of two parties always governs

questions.explanation: A two-party system is characterized by two dominant parties that regularly compete for power. Examples include the US (Democrats and Republicans) and UK (Conservatives and Labour).

questions.question: A one-party state is associated with:

options.B: a single party that holds a monopoly on political power and may ban other parties

questions.explanation: In a one-party state, a single party controls government and either bans or makes other parties ineffective, as seen in some communist and authoritarian states.

questions.question: The first political party in Nigeria was the:

options.B: Action Group (AG)
options.D: National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC)

questions.explanation: The Nigerian Youth Movement (NYM), founded in 1934, is generally regarded as Nigeria's first political party, though the NCNC (1944) was the first major nationalist party.

questions.question: The party that won the 1999 presidential election in Nigeria and formed the first civilian government after military rule was:

options.B: All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP)

questions.explanation: The Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) won the 1999 presidential election, with Olusegun Obasanjo becoming President. PDP dominated Nigerian politics from 1999 to 2015.

questions.question: The body responsible for conducting elections in Nigeria is the:

options.B: Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC)

questions.explanation: The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) is the constitutional body charged with conducting federal and state elections in Nigeria.

questions.question: Universal adult suffrage means the right to vote is extended to:

options.B: all adult citizens regardless of sex, race, wealth, or education

questions.explanation: Universal adult suffrage is the right of all adult citizens to vote without restrictions based on race, sex, wealth, religion, or literacy.

questions.question: The simple majority (first-past-the-post) electoral system means the winner must:

options.A: win more than 50% of total votes cast
options.B: win two-thirds of votes cast
options.C: get more votes than any other single candidate

questions.explanation: In the first-past-the-post (FPTP) system, the candidate with the most votes wins, even if they do not have an absolute majority (over 50%).

questions.question: Proportional representation (PR) is an electoral system designed to ensure that:

options.A: the winning party gets all seats
options.B: each party wins seats roughly in proportion to the votes it receives

questions.explanation: Proportional representation (PR) allocates legislative seats to parties in proportion to the total votes they receive, ensuring fairer representation of minority parties.

questions.question: Gerrymandering refers to the practice of:

options.A: allowing voters to vote multiple times
options.B: manipulating constituency boundaries to favour a particular party

questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the manipulation of electoral district boundaries to give one political party an unfair advantage over others in elections.

questions.question: The exclusive legislative list in Nigeria contains matters that can ONLY be legislated upon by:

options.B: Local Government Councils

questions.explanation: The exclusive legislative list (Second Schedule, 1999 Constitution) contains matters like defence, immigration, and currency that ONLY the National Assembly can legislate upon.

questions.question: The concurrent legislative list contains matters that:

options.A: only the federal government can legislate on
options.B: only state governments can legislate on
options.C: both federal and state governments can legislate on

questions.explanation: The concurrent legislative list contains matters (like education, health, and agriculture) on which both federal and state governments can legislate. Federal law prevails in case of conflict.

questions.question: The principle of derivation in Nigeria's revenue sharing means that:

options.A: revenue is shared equally among all states
options.B: states receive a portion of revenue based on the natural resources exploited from their territory
options.C: only oil-producing states receive federal funds
options.D: revenue goes entirely to the federal government

questions.explanation: Derivation ensures that states from which resources (mainly oil) are derived receive a percentage of the revenue from those resources (currently 13% for oil-producing states).

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of a federal state?

options.B: Supremacy of the central government on all matters
options.C: Division of powers between central and component units

questions.explanation: In federalism, powers are divided between central and state governments — neither is supreme on all matters. Central supremacy over all matters characterizes a unitary state.

questions.question: Nigeria currently has how many states?
questions.explanation: Nigeria currently has 36 states plus the Federal Capital Territory (FCT, Abuja). The states were created progressively from the original 3 regions at independence.

questions.question: Local government in Nigeria is the responsibility of which tier of government?

options.B: State government

questions.explanation: Under the 1999 Constitution, local government is primarily the responsibility of state governments, which create and oversee local government areas within their territories.

questions.question: The 1976 Local Government Reform in Nigeria was aimed at:

options.B: making local government a third tier of government with a uniform structure
options.C: transferring local government powers to the federal government

questions.explanation: The 1976 Local Government Reform under General Murtala/Obasanjo regime standardized local government across Nigeria, making it a recognized third tier with a uniform structure.

questions.question: Nigeria currently has how many local government areas (LGAs)?
questions.explanation: Nigeria has 774 Local Government Areas (LGAs) as provided in the 1999 Constitution. Each LGA has an elected council headed by an elected chairman.

questions.question: The Civil Service is characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: neutrality

questions.explanation: The civil service is characterized by permanence (continuous even when governments change), political neutrality, anonymity, and merit-based appointments. Civil servants are not partisan.

questions.question: Bureaucracy, as described by Max Weber, is characterized by:

options.B: hierarchy, impersonality, rules-based operation, and merit-based recruitment
options.C: elected officials making all administrative decisions

questions.explanation: Weber's ideal bureaucracy is characterized by: hierarchical structure, impersonality, written rules, merit-based appointment, specialization, and separation of officials from ownership of resources.

questions.question: The Federal Civil Service Commission in Nigeria is responsible for:

options.B: appointment, promotion, and discipline of senior federal civil servants

questions.explanation: The Federal Civil Service Commission oversees the appointment, promotion, transfer, and discipline of federal civil servants to ensure merit and impartiality.

questions.question: Which military leader headed Nigeria's government after the first coup of January 1966?

options.B: Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi

questions.explanation: Major General Johnson Aguiyi-Ironsi came to power after the January 15, 1966 coup. He was assassinated in the counter-coup of July 29, 1966.

questions.question: General Murtala Mohammed was assassinated during an abortive coup on:

options.B: February 13, 1976

questions.explanation: General Murtala Mohammed was assassinated on February 13, 1976, during a failed coup attempt led by Colonel Bukar Sukar Dimka. He was succeeded by General Olusegun Obasanjo.

questions.question: The military government that overthrew President Shehu Shagari's elected government in 1983 was led by:

options.B: Sani Abacha

questions.explanation: General Muhammadu Buhari led the coup of December 31, 1983, that overthrew President Shehu Shagari's Second Republic government. He ruled until August 1985 when Babangida ousted him.

questions.question: The annulment of the June 12, 1993 presidential election (widely believed to have been won by MKO Abiola) was carried out by:

options.B: General Ibrahim Babangida

questions.explanation: General Ibrahim Babangida annulled the June 12, 1993 presidential election results, widely believed to have been won by Chief MKO Abiola of the Social Democratic Party (SDP).

questions.question: Fundamental human rights in Nigeria are guaranteed under which chapter of the 1999 Constitution?

options.B: Chapter II

questions.explanation: Fundamental rights in Nigeria are contained in Chapter IV (Sections 33-46) of the 1999 Constitution.

questions.question: The right that protects individuals from being held in custody without charge or trial is:

options.B: Right to private and family life

questions.explanation: The right to personal liberty (Section 35, 1999 Constitution) protects individuals from unlawful arrest and detention. Police must charge or release a detainee within 24–48 hours.

questions.question: The principle of 'nemo judex in causa sua' means:

options.A: the accused is innocent until proven guilty
options.B: no one should be a judge in their own case (rule against bias)
options.C: everyone has a right to legal representation

questions.explanation: Nemo judex in causa sua is a natural justice principle meaning no person should be a judge in a case where they have a personal interest (conflict of interest).

questions.question: The principle of 'audi alteram partem' means:

options.B: hear the other side — both parties must be given a chance to present their case

questions.explanation: Audi alteram partem (hear the other side) is a fundamental rule of natural justice requiring that both sides in a dispute must be heard before a decision is made.

questions.question: Freedom of expression includes all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: freedom to incite violence or hatred
options.C: freedom to hold and express opinions
options.D: freedom to receive and impart information

questions.explanation: Freedom of expression does not include the right to incite violence, hatred, or cause public disorder. Like all rights, it has limitations to protect others' rights and public order.

questions.question: The United Nations (UN) was established in:
questions.explanation: The United Nations was established on October 24, 1945, after World War II, replacing the League of Nations. Its headquarters is in New York City.

questions.question: The principal organ of the UN responsible for maintaining international peace and security is the:

options.B: Security Council

questions.explanation: The UN Security Council has primary responsibility for maintaining international peace and security. It has 5 permanent members (P5) with veto power and 10 elected non-permanent members.

questions.question: The five permanent members of the UN Security Council with veto power are:

options.B: USA, UK, Germany, Japan, India
options.C: USA, Russia, China, Brazil, South Africa

questions.explanation: The five permanent members (P5) of the UN Security Council are the USA, United Kingdom, France, Russia, and China. Each has veto power over substantive resolutions.

questions.question: The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) was established in:
questions.explanation: ECOWAS was established on May 28, 1975, through the Treaty of Lagos, to promote economic integration among West African states. Nigeria was a founding member and key driver.

questions.question: The headquarters of ECOWAS is located in:

options.B: Accra, Ghana

questions.explanation: ECOWAS headquarters moved from Lagos to Abuja, Nigeria, when Abuja became Nigeria's capital. The ECOWAS Commission is based in Abuja.

questions.question: The African Union (AU) replaced which organization in 2002?

options.B: Organisation of African Unity (OAU)

questions.explanation: The African Union replaced the Organisation of African Unity (OAU) in 2002. The OAU was founded in 1963 and focused on decolonization; the AU has broader economic and political integration goals.

questions.question: The headquarters of the African Union is located in:

options.B: Nairobi, Kenya

questions.explanation: The headquarters of the African Union is in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. The AU Commission (secretariat) is also based there.

questions.question: NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization) is primarily a:

options.B: military alliance for collective defence

questions.explanation: NATO is a military alliance founded in 1949 based on the principle of collective defence (Article 5: an attack on one member is an attack on all). Its headquarters is in Brussels.

questions.question: Nigeria's foreign policy principle of 'Africa as the centerpiece' means that:

options.A: Nigeria focuses only on African affairs and ignores global issues
options.B: Africa and African interests are the central focus of Nigeria's foreign policy
options.C: Nigeria seeks to colonize African countries
options.D: only African countries can trade with Nigeria

questions.explanation: Nigeria's foreign policy has historically been Africa-centered — promoting African unity, liberation from colonialism, and African development as its primary diplomatic priority.

questions.question: The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) was established to:

options.A: create a military alliance against the USA
options.B: promote developing nations' interests independent of the Cold War blocs (USA and USSR)
options.C: establish free trade between African nations

questions.explanation: The Non-Aligned Movement (founded 1961) was created for developing nations to assert independence from both the Western (US-led) and Eastern (Soviet-led) blocs during the Cold War.

questions.question: Diplomacy is best defined as:

options.A: the use of military force to resolve conflicts
options.B: the practice of managing international relations through negotiation and peaceful means
options.D: the signing of trade agreements only

questions.explanation: Diplomacy is the art and practice of conducting negotiations between states to manage their relations, resolve conflicts, and pursue national interests through peaceful means.

questions.question: National interest refers to:

options.A: the personal interests of the President
options.B: the goals and ambitions that a state pursues in its relations with other states
options.C: interest rates set by the central bank

questions.explanation: National interest encompasses the goals, values, and priorities that guide a state's foreign policy decisions, including security, economic prosperity, and national prestige.

questions.question: Pressure groups differ from political parties in that pressure groups:

options.A: contest elections to win government power
options.B: seek to influence government policy without seeking to form a government

questions.explanation: Unlike political parties, pressure groups do not contest elections or seek to govern. They lobby government, raise public awareness, and advocate for specific interests or causes.

questions.question: The Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) is an example of which type of pressure group?

options.B: Sectional (interest) group

questions.explanation: The NLC is a sectional (interest) group representing the interests of workers. Its membership is limited to a particular section of society (workers). It lobbies for better wages and conditions.

questions.question: A promotional pressure group differs from a sectional group in that it:

options.B: promotes the interests of its members only
options.C: campaigns for causes that benefit all of society, not just its members

questions.explanation: Promotional (cause) groups advocate for broad causes benefiting society generally (e.g., environmental groups, human rights organisations) rather than narrow sectional interests.

questions.question: Public opinion refers to:

options.B: the collective attitudes and beliefs of the general population on political issues
options.D: the views of only educated citizens

questions.explanation: Public opinion is the aggregate of individual attitudes and beliefs held by the population on political, social, or other issues, often measured through polls and surveys.

questions.question: The mass media's role in shaping public opinion is called:

options.B: Propaganda

questions.explanation: Agenda-setting refers to the media's ability to influence which issues the public considers important by choosing what to cover extensively. The media 'sets the agenda' for public debate.

questions.question: Political socialization is the process by which:

options.B: individuals learn political values, beliefs, and behaviors from their society

questions.explanation: Political socialization is the lifelong process through which individuals acquire their political beliefs, values, and orientations from agents such as family, school, media, and peers.

questions.question: The most primary agent of political socialization is the:

options.B: Family

questions.explanation: The family is considered the most important primary agent of political socialization, as it is the first social institution a child encounters and shapes fundamental values and attitudes.

questions.question: Nigeria played a leading role in funding and establishing which peacekeeping force in West Africa?

options.B: ECOMOG

questions.explanation: Nigeria was the dominant force behind ECOMOG (ECOWAS Ceasefire Monitoring Group), which intervened in Liberia (1990) and Sierra Leone, demonstrating Nigeria's regional leadership.

questions.question: Citizenship by birth (jus soli) means:

options.B: citizenship obtained by being born in a particular country or to citizen parents

questions.explanation: Jus soli (right of the soil) grants citizenship to anyone born within a country's territory. Jus sanguinis (right of blood) grants citizenship based on parentage.

questions.question: Naturalization is the process by which:

options.B: a foreigner acquires citizenship of a country after meeting legal requirements
options.C: a child inherits citizenship from parents

questions.explanation: Naturalization is the legal process by which a non-citizen (foreigner) voluntarily acquires citizenship of a country, typically requiring residence, language proficiency, and an oath of allegiance.

questions.question: Which of the following is a civic duty of a Nigerian citizen?

options.B: Bribing electoral officials

questions.explanation: Voting in elections is a civic duty of citizens in a democracy. Other civic duties include paying taxes, obeying laws, and participating in community service.

questions.question: The principle that the majority should rule but the rights of minorities must be respected is called:

options.B: Majority tyranny

questions.explanation: In a democracy, decisions are made by majority rule, but this must be balanced with protection of minority rights to prevent 'tyranny of the majority.'

questions.question: Which of the following best describes representative democracy?

options.A: Citizens make all government decisions directly through referenda
options.B: Citizens elect representatives who make decisions on their behalf
options.C: A military council governs on behalf of citizens
options.D: One person rules on behalf of all citizens

questions.explanation: Representative (indirect) democracy involves citizens choosing elected representatives (legislators, president) to make decisions and govern on their behalf.

questions.question: The three arms of government are:

options.B: President, Parliament, Police

questions.explanation: The three arms of government are: (1) Legislature — makes laws, (2) Executive — implements laws, and (3) Judiciary — interprets laws and resolves disputes.

questions.question: Collective responsibility in a parliamentary cabinet system means that:

options.A: each minister is individually responsible to parliament
options.B: all cabinet ministers publicly support and defend cabinet decisions, even if they privately disagreed
options.C: the president takes responsibility for all decisions
options.D: civil servants are accountable for government decisions

questions.explanation: Collective cabinet responsibility means that all cabinet members are bound by cabinet decisions and must publicly support them. A minister who cannot support a decision must resign.

questions.question: Ballot stuffing is an electoral malpractice that involves:

options.B: illegally inserting pre-filled or extra ballot papers into ballot boxes
options.C: preventing voters from reaching the polls
options.D: changing the counting of valid votes

questions.explanation: Ballot stuffing involves fraudulently inserting multiple pre-completed ballot papers into a ballot box to inflate votes for a candidate or party.

questions.question: Vote buying is an electoral malpractice involving:

options.A: voters selling their voter registration cards
options.B: offering money, gifts, or other benefits to voters in exchange for their votes
options.C: buying election materials from the electoral commission
options.D: purchasing campaign time in the media

questions.explanation: Vote buying is when politicians or parties offer voters money, food, or gifts in exchange for voting for them. It undermines free and fair elections.

questions.question: Fascism is characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: one-party totalitarian rule
options.C: protection of individual rights and freedoms
options.D: glorification of the state and military

questions.explanation: Fascism (e.g., Nazi Germany, Mussolini's Italy) suppresses individual rights in favour of the state. It is characterized by extreme nationalism, totalitarianism, and dictatorial rule.

questions.question: Communism, as a political ideology, advocates for:

options.A: private ownership of the means of production
options.B: free market capitalism
options.C: collective/state ownership of means of production and a classless society

questions.explanation: Communism (as theorized by Marx and Engels) advocates for collective ownership of production, abolition of private property, and a classless, stateless society.

questions.question: Nigeria became a British colony through the amalgamation of the Northern and Southern Protectorates in:
questions.explanation: In 1914, Lord Frederick Lugard amalgamated the Northern and Southern Protectorates of Nigeria into a single entity called 'Nigeria' under British colonial rule.

questions.question: The policy of indirect rule in Nigeria was introduced and implemented by:

options.B: Sir Arthur Richards

questions.explanation: Lord Frederick Lugard introduced indirect rule in Northern Nigeria, governing through existing traditional rulers (emirs) rather than directly. He later applied it throughout Nigeria.

questions.question: Indirect rule was more successful in Northern Nigeria than the South because:

options.B: the North had centralized traditional authority structures (emirates) compatible with indirect rule
options.D: the South had no traditional rulers

questions.explanation: The centralized emirate system in Northern Nigeria (organized under Fulani emirs) was well-suited for indirect rule. Southern Nigeria had more decentralized and diverse political structures.

questions.question: Liberalism as a political ideology primarily advocates for:

options.B: individual freedoms, civil rights, democracy, and limited government

questions.explanation: Liberalism emphasizes individual liberty, civil rights, constitutional government, democracy, free markets, and protection of individual rights from government overreach.

questions.question: Conservatism as a political ideology generally favours:

options.B: state ownership of all industries
options.C: preservation of traditional institutions and gradual, cautious change

questions.explanation: Conservatism values tradition, gradual change, social order, established institutions, and is skeptical of rapid radical social transformation.

questions.question: Socialism advocates for:

options.A: complete private ownership of the economy
options.B: social ownership or regulation of the means of production in the interest of all

questions.explanation: Socialism advocates for collective or government ownership/regulation of the means of production and distribution to ensure a more equitable distribution of wealth.

questions.question: Delegated legislation refers to:

options.B: laws made by bodies other than the legislature, under powers delegated by parliament
options.D: decisions made by the Supreme Court

questions.explanation: Delegated (subsidiary) legislation is law made by ministers, local authorities, or other bodies under authority delegated to them by parliament (parent act). Examples include statutory instruments, by-laws.

questions.question: The Commonwealth of Nations is an association of:

options.B: former British colonies and territories that maintain voluntary ties
options.D: countries that share the same language

questions.explanation: The Commonwealth is a voluntary association of 54 countries, most of which are former territories of the British Empire, promoting democracy, development, and international peace.

questions.question: The Federal Character principle in Nigeria's 1999 Constitution requires that:

options.A: all government appointments go to the President's party
options.B: appointments and positions in government must reflect Nigeria's diverse national character
options.C: only northerners can head federal agencies

questions.explanation: The Federal Character principle (Section 14(3), 1999 Constitution) requires that federal appointments reflect the diversity of Nigeria's states and regions to promote national unity.

questions.question: The Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy are contained in which chapter of the 1999 Nigerian Constitution?

options.B: Chapter II

questions.explanation: Chapter II of the 1999 Constitution contains the Fundamental Objectives and Directive Principles of State Policy — goals the state should strive toward, though not directly enforceable in court.

questions.question: A state differs from a government in that:

options.A: a state is temporary while government is permanent
options.B: a state is permanent and sovereign while the government is its temporary agent
options.C: a state has a constitution but government does not
options.D: a state only exists in federal systems

questions.explanation: The state is permanent (a continuing political entity) while the government is the temporary agent that manages the state at any given time. Governments change; states continue.

questions.question: Which of the following is a quasi-judicial body in Nigeria?

options.B: The Federal Executive Council

questions.explanation: Quasi-judicial bodies exercise judicial-like functions without being formal courts. Examples include electoral tribunals, professional disciplinary panels, and administrative tribunals.

questions.question: A referendum is a device of:

options.A: representative democracy where legislators vote on bills
options.B: direct democracy where citizens vote directly on a specific issue

questions.explanation: A referendum is a form of direct democracy in which all eligible citizens vote directly on a specific constitutional, legislative, or policy question.

questions.question: Political culture refers to:

options.A: the cultural activities of political parties
options.B: the set of attitudes, beliefs, and values that citizens hold about their political system
options.C: the foreign cultural policies of government
options.D: the art and music of a nation

questions.explanation: Political culture encompasses the shared attitudes, beliefs, values, and orientations that citizens of a society hold toward their political system, institutions, and processes.

questions.question: An example of checks and balances in Nigeria's presidential system is:

options.A: the President appointing ministers without any approval
options.B: the Senate confirming presidential appointees
options.C: the President dissolving the National Assembly
options.D: judges being appointed by the legislature

questions.explanation: Senate confirmation of presidential nominees for ministers, ambassadors, and other key positions is a key example of legislative check on executive power in Nigeria.

questions.question: A by-election is held when:

options.A: the general election produces no winner
options.B: a legislative seat becomes vacant between general elections
options.C: two candidates tie in the general election
options.D: the president calls for a snap election

questions.explanation: A by-election (or bye-election) is held to fill a single vacant parliamentary or legislative seat that arises due to death, resignation, or disqualification of the sitting member.

questions.question: The Council of State in Nigeria is chaired by the:

options.B: Chief Justice

questions.explanation: The Council of State in Nigeria is chaired by the President. It includes the Vice President, former presidents, state governors, Senate President, and Speaker among others.

questions.question: The National Economic Council (NEC) in Nigeria is chaired by the:

options.B: Senate President

questions.explanation: The National Economic Council (NEC) is chaired by the Vice President and includes state governors and the Governor of the Central Bank. It advises on economic policy.

questions.question: The Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) Chairman is appointed by the:

options.B: President on the recommendation of the Council of State, subject to Senate confirmation

questions.explanation: The INEC Chairman is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Council of State and subject to confirmation by the Senate, to ensure some degree of independence.

questions.question: Nigeria's transition from military to civilian rule in 1999 led to the inauguration of which President?

options.B: Earnest Shonekan

questions.explanation: On May 29, 1999, Olusegun Obasanjo was inaugurated as President, marking Nigeria's return to civilian democratic rule after 16 years of military governance.

questions.question: The Interim National Government (ING) of Nigeria was headed by:

options.B: Ernest Shonekan

questions.explanation: Chief Ernest Shonekan headed the Interim National Government (ING) established on August 27, 1993, after Ibrahim Babangida's 'stepping aside.' It was overthrown by Sani Abacha on November 17, 1993.

questions.question: General Sani Abacha died in office on:

options.B: June 8, 1998

questions.explanation: General Sani Abacha died suddenly on June 8, 1998. He was succeeded by General Abdulsalami Abubakar, who initiated the transition to civilian rule completed in May 1999.

questions.question: Thomas Hobbes described life in the state of nature (before government) as:

options.B: solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short
options.D: governed by natural law and harmony

questions.explanation: In his work 'Leviathan' (1651), Hobbes described life without government as 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short,' which he used to justify absolute sovereign authority.

questions.question: John Locke's social contract theory differs from Hobbes in that Locke believed:

options.A: absolute monarchy is the best form of government
options.B: people surrender all their rights to the sovereign
options.C: government is based on consent and people retain natural rights; government that violates them can be overthrown
options.D: the state of nature was peaceful

questions.explanation: Locke argued that people retain natural rights (life, liberty, property) even after forming government. If government violates these rights, citizens have the right to rebel and change it.

questions.question: The State Independent Electoral Commission (SIEC) in Nigeria is responsible for conducting:

options.B: state governorship elections

questions.explanation: SIECs (State Independent Electoral Commissions) are established by state governments to conduct local government council elections within their respective states.

questions.question: Tribalism in Nigerian politics refers to:

options.B: the tendency to favour members of one's ethnic group in political appointments and voting
options.C: the use of traditional rulers in government

questions.explanation: Tribalism (ethnicity-based politics) refers to preferential treatment of one's own ethnic group in politics, appointments, and resource allocation, which is a major challenge to Nigerian unity.

questions.question: Nigeria's 1999 Constitution declares that Nigeria is a:

options.B: Islamic state

questions.explanation: Section 10 of the 1999 Constitution states that neither the Federal nor State governments shall adopt any religion as a state religion, making Nigeria officially a secular state.

questions.question: The institution responsible for auditing government accounts and reporting to the National Assembly in Nigeria is the:

options.B: Office of the Auditor General of the Federation

questions.explanation: The Auditor-General for the Federation audits federal government accounts and presents reports to the National Assembly to ensure financial accountability.

questions.question: To qualify as a presidential candidate in Nigeria, one must have at minimum a:

options.B: university degree

questions.explanation: Section 131(d) of the 1999 Constitution requires that a presidential candidate must have at least a School Certificate or its equivalent as the minimum educational qualification.

questions.question: The Berlin Conference of 1884-85 was significant for Africa because it:

options.B: partitioned Africa among European colonial powers
options.D: ended the slave trade in Africa

questions.explanation: The Berlin Conference (1884-85) formalized the 'Scramble for Africa' by establishing rules for European powers to partition the continent among themselves without African input.

questions.question: Treaties are binding agreements between:

options.B: sovereign states and/or international organizations

questions.explanation: Treaties are formal, legally binding agreements between sovereign states and/or international organizations, governed by international law.

questions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly in:
questions.explanation: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948, establishing a common standard of fundamental rights for all people.

questions.question: The Joint State-Local Government Account in Nigeria was established to:

options.A: allow states to tax local governments
options.B: pool allocations for states and local governments and ensure LGAs receive their statutory allocations

questions.explanation: The Joint Account pools federal allocations to states and local governments, requiring states to pass on the LGA share to local government councils, though abuses have occurred.

questions.question: Press freedom is important in a democracy because:

options.A: it allows media owners to become rich
options.B: it enables citizens to be informed and hold government accountable
options.C: it prevents the judiciary from making wrong decisions

questions.explanation: A free press informs citizens, exposes corruption and government abuses, facilitates public debate, and acts as a watchdog — essential functions in a democracy.

questions.question: A confederation differs from a federation in that in a confederation:

options.B: component units retain their sovereignty and the centre is weak

questions.explanation: In a confederation, sovereign member states voluntarily associate, retaining their sovereignty and delegating only limited powers to a weak central authority. The USA (1781-1789) was a confederation.

questions.question: The concept of 'divine right of kings' held that:

options.A: citizens have the right to overthrow kings
options.B: monarchs derive their right to rule directly from God and are not accountable to the people
options.C: kings must consult religious leaders before making decisions

questions.explanation: The divine right theory held that monarchs received their authority directly from God, making them accountable only to God and not to parliaments or people.

questions.question: The first Prime Minister of Nigeria at independence was:

options.B: Obafemi Awolowo

questions.explanation: Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa became Nigeria's first Prime Minister at independence on October 1, 1960. He was assassinated in the January 1966 coup.

questions.question: The first indigenous Governor-General (and first President) of Nigeria was:

options.B: Obafemi Awolowo

questions.explanation: Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe was the first indigenous Governor-General and then the first President of Nigeria when it became a republic in 1963.

questions.question: Nigeria's Second Republic (1979-1983) was characterized by which type of government?

options.B: Presidential system

questions.explanation: Nigeria's Second Republic (1979-1983) adopted a US-style presidential system under the 1979 Constitution, with Shehu Shagari as the first executive President.

questions.question: The abortive Third Republic of Nigeria was associated with which electoral system that used party primaries?

options.A: Two-party system with SDP and NRC
options.B: Multi-party system with unlimited parties

questions.explanation: For the abortive Third Republic, Babangida's military government created only two parties: the Social Democratic Party (SDP) and the National Republican Convention (NRC).

questions.question: Which Nigerian court has jurisdiction over disputes between the federation and a state, or between states?

options.B: Court of Appeal

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of Nigeria has original jurisdiction (hears cases first) in disputes between the federal government and a state, or between states.

questions.question: A recall in Nigerian politics refers to:

options.B: the process by which a constituency can remove their elected representative mid-term

questions.explanation: Recall is a constitutional mechanism allowing constituents to remove a sitting legislator before the end of their term through a petition signed by more than half of registered voters.

questions.question: Emergency powers in Nigeria can be declared by:

options.A: the President alone without any conditions
options.B: the President, subject to proclamation approved by the National Assembly

questions.explanation: Under Section 305 of the 1999 Constitution, the President may declare a state of emergency, but the proclamation must be approved by the National Assembly within two days.

questions.question: The International Court of Justice (ICJ) is the principal judicial organ of the:

options.B: European Union

questions.explanation: The International Court of Justice (ICJ), based in The Hague, Netherlands, is the principal judicial organ of the United Nations, settling disputes between states.

questions.question: The concept of 'power' in politics refers to:

options.B: the ability to influence or control the behavior of others
options.C: legal authority granted by the constitution only

questions.explanation: Political power is the ability or capacity to influence or determine the behavior, decisions, and actions of others, whether through force, authority, persuasion, or inducement.

questions.question: In Nigeria, election petitions challenging federal election results are filed at the:

options.B: Federal High Court

questions.explanation: Election petition tribunals handle election disputes. National Assembly petitions go to the Election Petition Tribunal; presidential disputes are initially heard by the Presidential Election Petition Tribunal.

questions.question: Constitutionalism refers to:

options.B: the principle that government authority must be exercised within constitutional limits

questions.explanation: Constitutionalism is the principle that government must operate within the framework of the constitution and that its powers are limited by constitutional rules and principles.

questions.question: The concept of 'nationalism' refers to:

options.A: loyalty to one's local government area
options.B: love and devotion to one's nation and the belief that the nation should govern itself
options.C: the economic policies of a nation

questions.explanation: Nationalism is a political ideology centered on the belief that people sharing common culture, language, or history should form their own sovereign state.

questions.question: The term 'fourth estate' is used to describe the:

options.B: traditional rulers

questions.explanation: The media is often called the 'fourth estate' (alongside the executive, legislature, and judiciary) because of its important role in informing the public and holding government accountable.

questions.question: The All Progressives Congress (APC) was formed in 2013 as a merger of which parties?

options.B: ACN, CPC, ANPP, and a faction of APGA

questions.explanation: The APC was formed in 2013 as a merger of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN), Congress for Progressive Change (CPC), All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP), and a faction of APGA.

questions.question: Transparency in government means:

options.A: government officials making decisions in secret
options.B: open, accessible governance where citizens can see how decisions are made and resources used
options.C: the government having clear glass buildings

questions.explanation: Transparency means government conducts its affairs openly, allowing citizens access to information about decision-making, spending, and policy implementation — essential for accountability.

questions.question: The Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) in Nigeria was established to:

options.B: investigate and prosecute economic and financial crimes including corruption

questions.explanation: The EFCC was established in 2003 to combat economic and financial crimes including advance fee fraud (419), money laundering, and corruption by public officials.

questions.question: The body that monitors compliance with the Federal Character principle in Nigeria is:

options.B: Federal Character Commission

questions.explanation: The Federal Character Commission (FCC) was established to ensure that federal appointments, employment, and distribution of resources reflect Nigeria's federal character (diversity).

questions.question: In Nigeria, the federal budget (Appropriation Bill) is prepared by the:

options.B: Federal Executive Council and then presented to the National Assembly for approval

questions.explanation: The executive prepares the budget (Appropriation Bill) which the President presents to a joint session of the National Assembly for consideration, amendment, and approval.

questions.question: Jean-Jacques Rousseau's social contract emphasized the concept of:

options.B: general will — the common good of all citizens

questions.explanation: Rousseau's social contract theory focused on the 'general will' — the collective decision of all citizens for the common good — as the basis of legitimate government.

questions.question: The concept of 'balance of power' in international relations refers to:

options.B: a distribution of power where no single state dominates all others
options.C: equal military strength among all nations

questions.explanation: Balance of power is a situation where power is distributed among states in a way that prevents any single state or coalition from dominating the international system.

questions.question: The Nigerian State Security Service (SSS) is primarily responsible for:

options.B: external military intelligence
options.C: internal security, counter-intelligence, and protecting national security

questions.explanation: The SSS (also known as DSS — Department of State Services) handles domestic intelligence, counter-espionage, and protecting Nigeria's internal security.

questions.question: The minimum age to vote in Nigerian elections is:

options.B: 18 years

questions.explanation: Section 77(2) of the 1999 Constitution sets the minimum voting age at 18 years. Citizens must also be registered voters with a Permanent Voter Card (PVC).

questions.question: To become President of Nigeria, a candidate must be at least:

options.B: 35 years old

questions.explanation: Section 131(b) of the 1999 Constitution requires that a presidential candidate must be at least 40 years old on the date of the election.

questions.question: Good governance is characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: rule of law

questions.explanation: Good governance requires transparency, accountability, rule of law, participation, efficiency, and equity. Arbitrary exercise of power is the antithesis of good governance.

questions.question: The 'Zungeru Conference' of 1950, which preceded the Macpherson Constitution, involved:

options.B: consultations with Nigerians on constitutional development

questions.explanation: The constitutional consultations leading to the 1951 Macpherson Constitution involved extensive consultations with Nigerians at village, district, regional, and national levels.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of local government in Nigeria?

options.A: Construction and maintenance of roads within the LGA
options.B: Collection of local taxes and rates

questions.explanation: Conducting federal elections is the responsibility of INEC, not local governments. Local governments are responsible for providing local services, collecting rates, and maintaining local infrastructure.

questions.question: Apartheid in South Africa was a system of:

options.B: institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination against non-white South Africans

questions.explanation: Apartheid (Afrikaans: 'separateness') was a system of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination in South Africa from 1948-1994, under which the white minority ruled.

questions.question: The Commonwealth of Nations has its secretariat in:

options.B: London, United Kingdom

questions.explanation: The Commonwealth Secretariat is headquartered at Marlborough House in London, United Kingdom.

questions.question: Propaganda differs from education in that propaganda:

options.B: seeks to manipulate or persuade people toward a particular viewpoint, often through selective or misleading information

questions.explanation: Propaganda uses biased or misleading information to promote a particular political cause or point of view, often suppressing opposing viewpoints. Education aims for truth and critical inquiry.

questions.question: A person who flees their country due to persecution and is granted protection in another country is called a:

options.B: Migrant worker

questions.explanation: A refugee is a person who has been forced to leave their country due to war, persecution, or natural disaster and has been granted international protection under the 1951 Refugee Convention.

questions.question: The Independent Corrupt Practices and Other Related Offences Commission (ICPC) was established in Nigeria in:
questions.explanation: The ICPC was established in 2000 under President Obasanjo to investigate and prosecute corruption, especially by public officials, and to educate about the dangers of corruption.

questions.question: The Action Group (AG) was founded in 1951 and was primarily associated with:

options.B: Nnamdi Azikiwe

questions.explanation: The Action Group (AG) was founded by Chief Obafemi Awolowo in 1951. It was based primarily in the Western Region and advocated for Yoruba interests and a federal system.

questions.question: The Northern People's Congress (NPC) was led by which prominent northern political figure?

options.B: Ahmadu Bello (Sardauna of Sokoto)

questions.explanation: The NPC was led by Sir Ahmadu Bello (the Sardauna of Sokoto). He was the Premier of the Northern Region. Tafawa Balewa was the NPC's parliamentary leader and Prime Minister.

questions.question: A plebiscite is a form of direct vote used specifically to:

options.B: determine the people's opinion on a territory's political status (e.g., independence or union)

questions.explanation: A plebiscite is a direct vote in which an entire electorate is invited to accept or refuse a specific proposal, particularly relating to political status (self-determination, independence).

questions.question: To amend the Nigerian Constitution (1999), a bill must be passed by:

options.A: a simple majority in the National Assembly
options.B: two-thirds of the National Assembly and at least two-thirds of all state houses of assembly

questions.explanation: Section 9 of the 1999 Constitution requires that amendments be passed by at least two-thirds of the National Assembly and approved by at least two-thirds (24) of the 36 state houses of assembly.

questions.question: The principle of 'innocent until proven guilty' (presumption of innocence) is a key element of:

options.B: the rule of law and fair trial

questions.explanation: The presumption of innocence is a fundamental right under the rule of law. Every accused person is considered innocent until the prosecution proves guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.

questions.question: The Ombudsman (Public Complaints Commission in Nigeria) is established to:

options.B: investigate complaints by citizens against government agencies and recommend remedies

questions.explanation: The Public Complaints Commission (Ombudsman) investigates citizens' complaints against government ministries and officials for maladministration, and recommends appropriate remedies.

questions.question: The World Trade Organization (WTO) regulates:

options.B: global trade rules between nations

questions.explanation: The WTO (founded 1995) is an international organization that regulates trade between nations, providing a framework for negotiating trade agreements and resolving disputes.

questions.question: The Crisis that led to the collapse of the First Republic in Nigeria (1964-1966) was primarily caused by:

options.B: electoral fraud, ethnic tensions, and political instability between parties and regions

questions.explanation: The First Republic collapsed due to disputed elections (1964 federal, 1965 Western Region), ethnic tensions, political violence, and governance crises that created the conditions for the January 1966 coup.

questions.question: An ex post facto law is one that:

options.B: retroactively criminalizes actions that were legal when committed

questions.explanation: An ex post facto law retroactively changes the legal consequences of actions committed before the law was passed. Most democracies prohibit such laws as they violate the rule of law.

questions.question: The concept of 'assimilation' in French colonial policy meant:

options.B: integrating colonized peoples into French culture and civilization

questions.explanation: French assimilation policy aimed to make colonized peoples 'French' by adopting French language, culture, and citizenship, contrasting with Britain's policy of indirect rule.

questions.question: OPEC (Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries) is an organization of:

options.B: oil-exporting countries that coordinate petroleum production policies

questions.explanation: OPEC is an intergovernmental organization of major oil-exporting nations that coordinates petroleum production and pricing policies to manage global oil markets.

questions.question: Political apathy refers to:

options.B: lack of interest, enthusiasm, or participation in politics

questions.explanation: Political apathy is the indifference or lack of interest in political affairs, resulting in low voter turnout, disengagement from civic activities, and withdrawal from the political process.

questions.question: The first African country to gain independence from colonial rule (south of the Sahara) was:

options.B: Ghana

questions.explanation: Ghana (formerly Gold Coast) became the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from colonial rule on March 6, 1957, under Kwame Nkrumah.

questions.question: The term of office of Nigerian state governors under the 1999 Constitution is:

options.B: four years, renewable once

questions.explanation: State governors serve a term of four years and may be re-elected for a maximum of two terms (8 years total), same as the President, under the 1999 Constitution.

questions.question: A quorum in the Nigerian Senate is achieved when at least how many senators are present?
questions.explanation: The quorum of the Nigerian Senate is one-third of its members (approximately 37 senators), as provided in the 1999 Constitution.

questions.question: Pan-Africanism as a political movement advocates for:

options.B: the unity and solidarity of all Africans and people of African descent
options.D: economic dependence on former colonial powers

questions.explanation: Pan-Africanism is a movement promoting the unity, solidarity, and liberation of all Africans and the African diaspora, associated with leaders like Kwame Nkrumah, W.E.B. Du Bois, and Marcus Garvey.

questions.question: Détente in the Cold War context refers to:

options.B: the easing of tension and improvement of relations between the USA and USSR

questions.explanation: Détente (1960s-1970s) was a period of relaxation of Cold War tensions between the USA and USSR, characterized by diplomatic engagement, arms control agreements, and reduced hostility.

questions.question: Checks and balances in government are designed to prevent:

options.B: concentration of too much power in any one branch or person

questions.explanation: Checks and balances distribute power among branches of government so that no single branch becomes too powerful, preventing tyranny and abuse of power.

questions.question: Which international organization monitors and promotes international nuclear safety and non-proliferation?

options.B: IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency)

questions.explanation: The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) promotes the peaceful use of nuclear energy and works to prevent nuclear weapons proliferation.

questions.question: The Permanent Voter Card (PVC) in Nigeria is issued by:

options.B: The Police Force

questions.explanation: The Permanent Voter Card (PVC) is issued by INEC (Independent National Electoral Commission) to registered voters as proof of registration and eligibility to vote.

questions.question: The National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) was established in Nigeria in:
questions.explanation: The NYSC was established by General Yakubu Gowon in 1973 (post-civil war) to promote national unity and integration by deploying graduates to serve in states outside their origins.

questions.question: The principle of 'one man, one vote' ensures:

options.B: each voter has equal voting power and influence
options.C: votes are cast only once per decade

questions.explanation: 'One man, one vote' (one person, one vote) ensures that each eligible voter has equal voting power — no person's vote counts more than another's.

questions.question: The concept of 'locus standi' in law refers to:

options.A: the right of a person to bring a case before a court because they have a sufficient interest
options.B: the location where a trial is held
options.C: the right to remain silent during interrogation
options.D: the length of a prison sentence

questions.explanation: Locus standi (Latin: 'place to stand') refers to the legal capacity or right of a person or organization to initiate legal proceedings in court, based on having a sufficient direct interest in the matter.

questions.question: The World Health Organization (WHO) is a specialized agency of the:

options.B: European Union

questions.explanation: The WHO is a specialized agency of the United Nations responsible for international public health. It coordinates responses to global health emergencies and sets health standards.

questions.question: The 'Aburi Accord' of January 1967 was an agreement between Yakubu Gowon and Ojukwu aimed at:

options.B: resolving the political crisis and agreeing on a loose confederation to keep Nigeria together

questions.explanation: The Aburi Accord (Aburi, Ghana, January 1967) was an agreement between Gowon and Ojukwu for a loose confederation, but it was not fully implemented by the federal side, contributing to the civil war.

questions.question: Coup d'état literally means:

options.B: a sudden seizure of state power, typically by the military
options.C: declaration of a state of emergency

questions.explanation: Coup d'état (French: 'blow of state') refers to the sudden, forcible seizure of power from a government, typically carried out by the military or a small group with access to force.

questions.question: Civil society organizations (CSOs) contribute to democracy by:

options.B: organizing coups against elected governments
options.C: advocating for citizens' rights, promoting accountability, and bridging government-citizen relations

questions.explanation: Civil society organizations (NGOs, trade unions, professional associations, community groups) advocate for rights, hold government accountable, and represent citizens' interests outside the formal political system.
